sourceText: This reflects the belief that religious revelation (the Qur'an) and the natural world (science and reason) both originate from God and therefore must be in harmony. It is an assertion that faith and reason are compatible.
I experienced the pain of separation and identity crisis, having been taken from my family at a young age under the Devşirme system to serve as a soldier in a foreign land.
I discovered my true talents by honing my engineering and architectural skills during military service, which earned me the Sultan's trust and allowed me to become the empire's chief architect.
Architecture begins with fulfilling a need, and ends as an art that expresses faith and authority.
This reflects his philosophy that the essence of architecture must transcend practical purposes to become a symbol of the era's spirit, faith, and power.
Under a perfect dome, there must be a space without pillars, where all souls can become one without obstruction.

This emphasizes the importance of the vast, unified interior spaces he pursued. It shows his intention to create not just a physical structure, but a space for the spiritual unification of the community.
My work as an apprentice was the Şehzade Mosque in Istanbul, my work as a journeyman was the Süleymaniye Mosque, and my masterpiece as a master is the Selimiye Mosque in Edirne.
Sinan himself evaluated his career in three stages. This demonstrates his craftsmanship and his spirit of striving for perfection through continuous learning and development.

As a wandering scholar, al-Hamadani constantly faced intense rivalries with established literary figures in the courts he visited. He had to perpetually prove his genius through public debates and literary contests to secure the patronage necessary for his survival and work.

He overcame these challenges through sheer intellectual brilliance and innovation. By inventing the Maqama, a completely new and captivating literary form, he created a niche that no rival could contest, securing his legacy as 'Badi' al-Zaman,' the Wonder of the Age.
Let me fleece this flock, before they get away.
A cynical but witty observation on seizing opportunities, characteristic of his roguish protagonist.
